2604.06071.title: Stories of Your Life as Others: A Round-Trip Evaluation of LLM-Generated Life Stories Conditioned on Rich Psychometric Profiles
2604.06071.paper_summary: Personality traits are richly encoded in natural language, and large language models (LLMs) trained on human text can simulate personality when conditioned on persona descriptions. However, existing evaluations rely predominantly on questionnaire self-report by the conditioned model, are limited in architectural diversity, and rarely use real human psychometric data. Without addressing these limitations, it remains unclear whether personality conditioning produces psychometrically informative representations of individual differences or merely superficial alignment with trait descriptors. To test how robustly LLMs can encode personality into extended text, we condition LLMs on real psychometric profiles from 290 participants to generate first-person life story narratives, and then task independent LLMs to recover personality scores from those narratives alone. We show that personality scores can be recovered from the generated narratives at levels approaching human test-retest reliability (mean r = 0.750, 85% of the human ceiling), and that recovery is robust across 10 LLM narrative generators and 3 LLM personality scorers spanning 6 providers. Decomposing systematic biases reveals that scoring models achieve their accuracy while counteracting alignment-induced defaults. Content analysis of the generated narratives shows that personality conditioning produces behaviourally differentiated text: nine of ten coded features correlate significantly with the same features in participants' real conversations, and personality-driven emotional reactivity patterns in narratives replicate in real conversational data. These findings provide evidence that the personality-language relationship captured during pretraining supports robust encoding and decoding of individual differences, including characteristic emotional variability patterns that replicate in real human behaviour.
2604.06071.paper_summary_zh: 個性特徵在自然語言中被豐富地編碼，而基於人類文本訓練的大型語言模型（LLMs）在依據角色描述進行調整時，可以模擬個性。然而，現有的評估主要依賴於調整模型的問卷自我報告，架構多樣性有限，且很少使用真實的人類心理測量數據。在不解決這些限制的情況下，仍然不清楚個性調整是否產生了心理測量上具有信息量的個體差異表徵，或僅僅是與特徵描述的表面對齊。為了測試LLMs能夠多大程度上將個性編碼進擴展文本中，我們基於290名參與者的真實心理測量檔案對LLMs進行調整，以生成第一人稱的生活故事敘述，然後讓獨立的LLMs僅從這些敘述中恢復個性分數。我們顯示，從生成的敘述中可以恢復個性分數，其水平接近於人類測試的重測信度（平均r = 0.750，達到人類上限的85%），並且在10個LLM敘述生成器和3個LLM個性評分器之間的恢復是穩健的，涵蓋了6個供應商。對系統性偏差的分解顯示，評分模型在抵消對齊引起的默認情況下達到了其準確性。對生成敘述的內容分析顯示，個性調整產生了行為上有差異的文本：十個編碼特徵中有九個與參與者的真實對話中的相同特徵顯著相關，且敘述中的個性驅動的情感反應模式在真實對話數據中得以複製。這些發現提供了證據，表明在預訓練期間捕捉的個性-語言關係支持了個體差異的穩健編碼和解碼，包括在真實人類行為中複製的特徵性情感變異模式。
2604.06071.author: Ben Wigler et.al.
2604.06071.authors: Ben Wigler, Maria Tsfasman, Tiffany Matej Hrkalovic
